questions.explanation: CloudWatch Logs Insights uses its own query language with commands: filter, parse, stats, sort, limit, and display. Lambda REPORT lines contain the literal text 'REPORT' in @type (when parsed by Logs Insights) or in @message. The parse command extracts a named field from a log message using a glob pattern (asterisk for wildcard). Option C correctly: filters for REPORT lines, extracts duration as a numeric field using a glob pattern, then filters for durations over 10,000. Options A and D use SQL and CloudWatch Metrics Insights syntax respectively.
questions.takeaway: CloudWatch Logs Insights syntax: filter (where clause), parse (extract fields from text), stats (aggregations), sort, limit. Use parse with glob patterns to extract numeric values from unstructured Lambda log lines.

questions.prompt: A Java Lambda function has a 10-second cold start due to Spring framework initialization and extensive class loading. The team wants cold starts under 1 second without using provisioned concurrency. Which Lambda feature addresses this?

options.text: Lambda Extensions — attach a warm-up extension to pre-load the Spring application context
Lambda SnapStart — snapshots the initialized execution environment for fast restore on cold start
Lambda Layers — package Spring libraries separately to speed up class loading
ARM/Graviton2 architecture — Java initialization is significantly faster on ARM processors

questions.explanation: Lambda SnapStart (available for Java runtimes with corretto-11, corretto-17, corretto-21) takes a snapshot of the fully initialized execution environment after the init phase completes. On subsequent cold starts, Lambda restores from the cached snapshot instead of re-running initialization — typically reducing Java cold start latency from seconds to under 200ms. Provisioned concurrency (which the question excludes) keeps environments permanently warm. Lambda Layers reduce package size but don't snapshot the JVM state. ARM architecture improves compute throughput but doesn't eliminate 10-second initialization.
questions.takeaway: Lambda SnapStart (Java): snapshot initialized JVM state → restore on cold start instead of reinitializing. Reduces Java cold starts from seconds to milliseconds without provisioned concurrency costs.

questions.prompt: An application reads product details from RDS and caches them in ElastiCache. When a product is updated in RDS, the cache should immediately reflect the new value so subsequent reads never return stale data. Which caching strategy achieves this?

options.text: Lazy loading (cache-aside) — populate cache only on cache misses
Write-through — update both the cache and the database on every write
Read-through — the cache library fetches from the database automatically on cache miss
TTL-based expiration — set a short TTL so stale data expires quickly

questions.explanation: Write-through caching updates the cache simultaneously with every database write. After each RDS update, the application writes the new value to both RDS and ElastiCache — keeping them perfectly synchronized. Subsequent reads always get the current value from cache. Lazy loading only populates cache on a miss, meaning stale data can persist until evicted or expired. Read-through is cache-controlled lazy loading — same stale data risk. TTL expiration is a mitigation, not immediate consistency; stale data persists until TTL expires.
questions.takeaway: Write-through = cache and DB always in sync (no stale reads) at the cost of added write latency. Lazy loading = potential stale reads but lower write overhead. Choose based on consistency requirements.

questions.prompt: A developer wants to trigger a PagerDuty alert only when BOTH the Lambda error rate AND the DynamoDB throttle count are elevated simultaneously, to reduce false-positive alerts caused by individual metric spikes. Which CloudWatch feature supports this?

options.text: CloudWatch Anomaly Detection alarms configured on both metrics
A CloudWatch composite alarm combining the Lambda and DynamoDB metric alarms with an AND condition
CloudWatch Contributor Insights to correlate Lambda errors with DynamoDB throttle events
Two separate SNS topics with a downstream Lambda function that checks both conditions before paging

questions.explanation: CloudWatch composite alarms combine multiple alarms (metric alarms or other composite alarms) using boolean logic (AND, OR, NOT). Configuring a composite alarm that fires only when BOTH the Lambda error alarm AND the DynamoDB throttle alarm are in ALARM state eliminates false positives from isolated metric spikes. Anomaly Detection identifies unusual patterns in individual metrics but doesn't correlate conditions. Contributor Insights analyzes log patterns. A custom Lambda is a valid workaround but adds operational complexity.
questions.takeaway: CloudWatch composite alarms with AND logic reduce alarm noise by firing only when multiple correlated conditions are simultaneously true — the right tool for correlation-based alerting.

questions.prompt: A high-traffic Lambda function receives 5,000 requests per second. Tracing every request with X-Ray would be cost-prohibitive. What X-Ray feature controls how many requests are sampled for tracing?

options.text: CloudWatch metric filters on the Lambda log group to sample log events
X-Ray sampling rules that define a reservoir (minimum per second) and a fixed rate (percentage above reservoir)
Lambda concurrency limits to reduce the number of traced invocations
X-Ray groups to filter which stored traces are displayed in the Service Map

questions.explanation: X-Ray sampling rules control which requests are traced and at what volume. Each rule defines: a reservoir (guaranteed minimum number of traces per second regardless of rate) and a fixed rate (percentage of requests above the reservoir to trace). The default rule samples 5% with a 1 request/second reservoir. Custom rules can target specific services, HTTP methods, URL paths, or resource ARNs. Lambda concurrency limits affect performance, not tracing. X-Ray groups filter the console view of already-stored traces, not the sampling decision.
questions.takeaway: X-Ray sampling rules: reservoir (minimum traces/sec guaranteed) + fixed rate (% above reservoir). Balance observability coverage against cost by tuning per-service rules. Default: 5% rate, 1/sec reservoir.

questions.prompt: A developer suspects a small number of DynamoDB partition keys are responsible for most of the table's read throttling. Which AWS observability feature identifies the top contributing partition keys by request volume without requiring custom log analysis?

options.text: DynamoDB Streams with a Lambda consumer that counts accesses per partition key
CloudWatch Contributor Insights for DynamoDB — natively identifies top partition keys by request count
AWS X-Ray traces on the application code that calls DynamoDB, grouped by partition key value
CloudTrail data events for DynamoDB, analyzed with Athena to aggregate by partition key

questions.explanation: CloudWatch Contributor Insights for DynamoDB is a native AWS feature that analyzes DynamoDB request traffic and automatically surfaces the top contributors (most-accessed partition keys, most-throttled keys, most common errors) by volume — in near real-time. It requires no custom code, log analysis, or infrastructure. Enabling it on a table provides immediate visibility into hot key patterns. DynamoDB Streams + Lambda requires building a custom aggregation pipeline. X-Ray traces DynamoDB at the SDK call level but doesn't aggregate by key at scale. CloudTrail + Athena works but requires significant setup and query writing.
questions.takeaway: CloudWatch Contributor Insights for DynamoDB natively identifies hot partition keys and top access patterns — enable it to diagnose throttling root causes before redesigning your key schema or adding caching.
